title: Unplanned Physical Security Incidents at Transmission Sites

compliance.assurance_rationale: Uplifted from contributing to partial: UC provides meaningful monitoring evidence for CIP-014-3 R3 Part 3.1.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that NERC CIP CIP-014-3 R3 Part 3.1 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.13.70: Unplanned Physical Security Incidents at Transmission Sites.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.13.70' running on index=physical sourcetype=transmission:perimeter, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

description: We centralize alarms for intrusion, cut fence, or camera loss at transmission facilities for timely response and regulatory awareness.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for NERC CIP compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes physical and sourcetypes transmission:perimeter, transmission:perimeter`. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Map alarm feeds to station registry. (2) Page security dispatch on critical sites. (3) Correlate with law enforcement case numbers in ITSM. (4) Feed significant events to CIP-008 evaluation. Indexes required: `index=physical`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=transmission:perimeter`, `sourcetype=transmission:perimeter``; Lookups: `cip014_station_register`.
visualization: Timeline (alarms), Table (by station), Single value (critical alarms). Key fields: `alarm_type`, `criticality`, `first`, `last`, `station_id`.
knownFalsePositives: Planned maintenance, registered change tickets, and approved work can look like noncompliance in raw telemetry; confirm against the current change record before you escalate.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk OT Security Add-on (5151).
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `index=physical` `sourcetype=transmission:perimeter`.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Map alarm feeds to station registry. (2) Page security dispatch on critical sites. (3) Correlate with law enforcement case numbers in ITSM. (4) Feed significant events to CIP-008 evaluation.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=physical sourcetype=transmission:perimeter earliest=-7d (alarm_type="intrusion" OR alarm_type="fence_cut" OR alarm_type="video_loss")
| lookup cip014_station_register station_id OUTPUT criticality
| stats count earliest(_time) as first latest(_time) as last by station_id, alarm_type, criticality
| sort - count
```

Understanding this SPL

**Unplanned Physical Security Incidents at Transmission Sites (CIP-014-3 R3 Part 3.1)** — We centralize alarms for intrusion, cut fence, or camera loss at transmission facilities for timely response and regulatory awareness.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=physical` `sourcetype=transmission:perimeter`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk OT Security Add-on (5151). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: physical; **sourcetype**: transmission:perimeter. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=physical, sourcetype=transmission:perimeter, time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• Enriches events using `lookup` (lookup definition + optional OUTPUT fields).
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by station_id, alarm_type, criticality** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.


Step 3 — Validate
Compare with the ESP or perimeter firewall log (for example Palo Alto or FortiGate) for the same window. Spot-check a few source events, reconcile counts, and verify field extractions, thresholds, and index permissions for the people who need this evidence.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Timeline (alarms), Table (by station), Single value (critical alarms)
grandmaExplanation: We centralize alarms for intrusion, cut fence, or camera loss at transmission facilities for timely response and regulatory awareness. That is important for the big power grid, where proof needs to line up with what the rules expect.
